videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Cape Town, and in this video we're going to show you our top things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Located at the southwestern tip of South Africa, Cape Town is a diverse city known for its stunning natural beauty, rich history, and vibrant culture. Surrounded by the iconic Table Mountain and the blue waters of the Atlantic Ocean, Cape Town offers a unique mix of urban sophistication and outdoor adventure. In this video, we explore our favorite things to do in and around Cape Town: from touristic highlights to exciting nature adventures, and hidden gem attractions. You can find links to all the activities and tours that we mention in the description below the video. At the end, we also share important travel tips that will help you plan your trip. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Cape Town has a captivating history that stretches over centuries. It was the center of European colonization and has a melting pot of cultures as a result of the slave trade. It also survived the grueling Apartheid, a racial segregation that lasted more than 40 years. Today, Cape Town has a number of museums and memorials remembering its past. These include the Company's Garden, Parliament, the Slave Lodge, Green Market Square, and the Castle of Good Hope. To truly understand this turbulent history, we recommend going on a guided walking tour. The Sea Point promenade is a safe place to ride a bicycle in Cape Town since it's flat and there are no cars to worry about. There's a lot to see and do along this route. You can rent a bike with Up Cycles from any of their three locations: the V&A Waterfront, Camps Bay, or the Sea Point Pavilion. Rentals are drop-and-go, meaning you can ride one-way without having to return to the same location. We rented our bikes at the Pavilion and enjoyed a relaxing 4.5 km ride along the promenade to the bustling V&A Waterfront. During the Apartheid years, around 60,000 residents of District 6 were forcibly removed away from the city center by the government. The District 6 Museum was established as a memorial to the vibrant community that once thrived in this area. You can find personal stories, photographs, and artifacts that highlight the resilience and spirit of its former inhabitants. You can also join a short guided tour by an ex-resident storyteller at different times of the day. Situated in a picturesque location with stunning views of Table Mountain, the V&A Waterfront is a bustling zone of entertainment. It's filled with a wide range of shops, from high-end luxury brands to local boutiques as well as world-class restaurants and bars. Popular attractions here include the Cape Wheel, the Two Oceans Aquarium, the Diamond Museum, and a variety of sunset cruises. You might even be able to spot some wildlife. So here at the waterfront, if you want to spot some South African fur seals, uh, you can come to the Robben Island Ferry area and there's just a little platform. They're also swimming around in the water here. South Africa is known to be a passionate sports country with rugby, cricket, and soccer being the main ones played and watched. Hence, catching a game in Cape Town is an experience that should not be missed by any sports enthusiasts. We watched a cricket match in the iconic Newlands Cricket Ground and learned a lot about the sport. It also gave us a great feel of the local culture. Alternatively, you can catch a rugby match or soccer game at the Cape Town Stadium, which is closer to the city center. Chapman's Peak Drive is a 9 km scenic toll road near Cape Town, known for its breathtaking curves and cliffside views. Winding between Hout Bay and Noordhoek, it offers panoramas of the ocean and mountains, with some viewpoints reaching over 500 m above sea level. The road is narrow and soars high above jagged cliffs, so it's best to take in the stunning views at a slow pace. We recommend driving this route by rental car and combining it with other activities on the Cape Peninsula, which we'll highlight later in this video. Located in the heart of Cape Town, Bo-Kaap is the city's most iconic neighborhood. Hilly streets are lined with vibrantly colored houses and mosques. Cape Town's Muslim Community mainly resides in this area and they're often referred to as Cape Malays. Many of the families were brought here by Dutch settlers from Indonesia and Malaysia as well as various African countries in the 18th century. Walking up and down the streets is an experience, but you can also explore the Bo-Kaap Museum or savor authentic Cape Malay cuisine. Make sure to respect the residents privacy in this community. For centuries Robben Island served as a harsh prison, famously holding Nelson Mandela for 18 years. It has since been transformed into UNESCO World Heritage Site and offers a crucial glimpse into South Africa's past. A visit to the island starts with a 30 to 60-minute ferry boat ride from Nelson Mandela Gateway at the V&A Waterfront. On the island, you'll get on a tour bus that drives you to the prison, Mandela's cell, and the lime quarry where prisoners were forced to break rocks. Former inmates of the prison guide the tour, sharing their experiences and offering powerful insights into the times of Apartheid. The tour only departs three to four times per day, so it's best to book your tickets in advance. Cape Town is bursting with vibrant food markets, each offering unique flavors and experiences. For a trendy international vibe, head to the Time Out Market. Housed in a historic building, this is a gourmet haven, boasting over 20 food stalls and serving both local and international cuisine. But for a more laid-back community feel, explore the Oranjezicht City Farm Market. Here, you'll find a curated selection of fresh produce, artisanal cheeses, and delicious treats. Mingle with locals while you sip craft coffee and soak up the peaceful atmosphere. Located about an hour's drive from Cape Town, the Cape Winelands is a beautiful region known for its world-class wines, quaint towns, and stunning natural beauty. The heart of the region lies in the towns of Paarl, Stellenbosch, and Fraschhoek, each boasting unique character and an abundance of wineries to explore. The Franschhoek Wine Tram is a hop-on hop-off experience that offers a nice way to discover this area. During the tour, you'll have have time to sample some of the delicious wines that come from this region. You can spend the day strolling the vineyards, taking cellar tours, and enjoying drinking and dining. Another must-do day trip is to the scenic Cape Peninsula. This dramatic stretch of land offers stunning scenery and diverse experiences, which can easily be reached by car. You can drive via Chapman's Peak to the African Penguin Colony at Boulders Beach. This stretch of beach charges an entrance fee to access it, but seeing so many penguins waddling around in one place is definitely worth the price. Alternatively, you can head to one of the public beaches to the south where you can also spot some penguins for free. Continue driving to the Cape of Good Hope for jaw-dropping coastal scenery and hiking trails. If you don't have a rental car, it's also possible to book a guided day tour for the Cape Peninsula. Find a link to that tour in the description below the video. Soaring 1,087 m above the city center, the flat-topped Table Mountain is the most famous landmark in Cape Town. There are several ways to summit this iconic mountain. The most rewarding but also challenging way is by hiking one of the trails. Platteklip Gorge is the most popular hike, providing a direct but steep route to the summit. It takes about 2 to 3 hours to zigzag up to the top. Kasteelspoort is another common trail but starts from Camps Bay. We hiked down this way from the top of Table Mountain as there's more elevation. But if you're short on time or want to have a more relaxing experience, you can also reach the summit by cable car. The views from the top are rewarding either way. Located on the slopes of Table Mountain, Kristenbosch Botanical Gardens houses a variety of native plants, flowers, shrubs, and trees, which provide year- round blossoms in color. You can explore well-marked trails through the forested area and even hike to the summit of Table Mountain via the Skeleton Gorge Trail. In summertime, the gardens also host outdoor music events, known as the the Kirstenbosch Summer Sunset Concerts. Plan for at least 3 to 4 hours to wander around the gardens as they're very large. Make sure you also visit the Canopy Walk here because it offers some amazing views of, uh, Table Mountain in the back. Cape Town has some of the most alluring beaches in all of South Africa. There are many to choose from, but here are our favorite ones. Hout Bay is a picturesque fishing village with a charming harbor. Bloubergstrand is a haven for water sports enthusiasts, renowned for its strong winds that are ideal for kite surfing and surfing. Bakoven Beach is a tranquil escape from the bustling city, with secluded coves and gorgeous sunset views. Camps Bay is near Bakoven and is a popular spot for locals and tourists alike, offering trendy restaurants, and a palm-lined promenade. Our favorite thing to do in Cape Town is hiking Lion's Head at sunset. As the the trail is just over 4 km long with an elevation gain of 330 meters, this hike is less demanding than its neighbor of Table Mountain. The hike is absolutely stunning from start to finish, with sweeping views over the Atlantic Ocean, the city center, and of course Table Mountain. While the first few kilometers are fairly easy, the last section is more rocky and challenging. For more advanced hikers, you can take a series of ladders and chains to the top. However, this section can be bypassed to the left if it's too difficult, and you only have to take one ladder if you choose that option. The trail can get very windy near the top so be careful. Also expect crowds on a clear day. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share a few travel tips for Cape Town. First, consider downloading the Uber App for convenient, safe, and affordable transportation around Cape Town. Alternatively, having a rental car can also provide flexibility in exploring the surrounding areas. We used rentalcars.com to book our car. Second, you should be prepared for load shedding. This is a planned power outage that occurs in South Africa to manage demand for electricity. Download the ESP App to stay updated on scheduled blackouts for your area. Having a power bank for your phone and a headlamp will also be helpful during the power outages. While Cape Town is a beautiful destination, it's important to stay alert and be cautious. Avoid displaying expensive items, be aware of your surroundings, and stick to well-lit and populated areas, especially at night. As a general travel rule, avoid Cape Town's Township areas. For easy access to popular attractions and beaches, consider staying in areas along the coastline between the V&A Waterfront and Bakoven. If you're looking for a budget-friendly option, Sea Point is a central location that offers more affordable accommodations. Before booking a stay, always do your research to decide what area best fits your needs and expectations. You can find a few accommodation options and safe areas in the description below the video. but once the people of this community found Freedom they painted the houses in bright and colorful Hues to symbolize that freedom it's a very iconic place to explore here in Cape Town Cape Malay food is by far our favorite cuisine in South Africa and eating it in Bo Kaap is the most authentic way to experience it you have to head to either Bo Kaap Kombuis for dinner or faeeza's home kitchen anytime during the day for a quick [Music] bite day seven this is our adrenaline junkie day if you're not one of those don't worry we'll share some Alternatives with you Cape Town is an Adrenaline Junkies Heaven we've got such epic things to do from Ab sailing Table Mountain to paragliding off Signal Hill to Swimming with Sharks to snorkeling with seals the options are endless you're going to start your day with breakfast on Kloof Street in Gardens there are tons of lovely cafe's to choose from from there head up Signal Hill for the flight of your life you're going to be paragliding right over the Atlantic Seaboard down onto the promenade but don't worry the landing is super chilled oh my gosh I actually didn't know what to expect but that was some of the most incredible scenery we flew over the ocean and I'm pretty sure I still a dolphin like oh my gosh guys you really really have to do this after your flight booking afternoon of swimming with seals you have a short 30-minute drive to Hout Bay but be warned the sea can be rough on certain days so pack motion sickness pills if you're prone to that if you decide to do the Shark cage diving on this day instead then leave early as you'll have a bit of a drive ahead of you down the coast to a town called Gansbaai where the great whites sometimes hang out alternatively if you're not a junkie hire a car and do a short road trip for breakfast or lunch to one of the many cute towns nearby some of our favorites are St Helena Bay Paternoster langebaan for greece Vibes Kalk Bay for cute coffee shops or hermanus which is super famous for the whale watching during the months of June to [Music] December day eight we recommend spending this day in one of the renowned wine regions of South Africa stellenboisch Paarl Constanta and franschoek are among the best we suggest is doing the latter franschoek which means French Corner in Afrikaans and refers to the Hugo settlers who fleeing religious persecution in 17th century France found solace in this idelic Valley situated about an hour drive from the center of Cape town franschoek is known for its incredible food and rolling Vineyards the most popular thing to do in this little town by far is the wine tram which was designed specifically for tourists to experience tastings at a number of Vineyards welcome to the Franschoek wine tram it is a Must do in Cape Town if you're a big fan of wine and really really good food you'll spend most of the day on the tram getting crunk please pace yourself because the wine here is pretty strong and of course plentiful franschoek is also known as the culinary capital of the cape so be sure to make a reservation at one of the outstanding restaurants and if you're really feeling the effects of the wine then we suggest spending a night here we stayed at Farm Sanctuary home to the famous painting Pig Pigcasso which also happens to be one of the stops on the pink tram route day nine South Africa has a rich but pretty dark history and Cape Town in particular has so many different museums to visit we suggest spending a day learning more about the history and local culture here while Cape Town is very beautiful it's also got a lot of inequality and often tourists don't get to experience the authentic and very real way the majority of South Africans live here a Township tour is a great way to learn about the vibrant communities that play a role in the city's history and continue to shape its cultural landscape something else you can do is a trip across the bay to Robben Island you can usually see it but today it's covered in Mist but this is where former president Nelson Mandela was imprisoned for 18 years and is a significant place to visit if you love history the full tour including the ferry ride from the V&A takes around 3 and 1/2 hours we recommend you book this one in person at the Gateway to Robben Island Building or on their website next up is the heart of Cape Town Museum where you'll learn about how Christian Barnard a South African cardiac surgeon performed the world's very first human to human heart transplant operation just remarkable we're so proud and finally the Ziets Mocca Museum of Contemporary Art it's gorgeous and worth a visit if you're appreciate art as well day 10 and it's the end of your time here in the mother City you're either off home or if you're lucky onto the Garden Route or one of the national parks which we actually recommend if not you'll still go home with the best memories ever and we are 100% certain that you'll be back for more on the last day we recommend strolling around the V&A Waterfront there are many markets and curio shops you can explore to find some cute little nik-naks to take home and of course to support our awesome local Artisans don't forget to grab our Cape Town resource pack before you start to plan we hope you found this video super helpful and if you did we'd appreciate if you could give it a like it helps more people see the video safe travels and we'll see you in the next one bye [Music] bye [Music]

the most beautiful areas in Cape Town and where we currently live the Atlantic Seaboard is located here on the western side of the city as such it is the best place to be if you like watching sunset what we mean when we touch on some of the other areas later in the video there are a number of suburbs which make up the Atlantic Seaboard let's start with Sea Point by far our favorite place and where we currently call home the area has a lively atmosphere and a wide range of accommodation options from funky co- living spots comfortable hotels luxury hotels to air bnbs if you decide to stay in Sea Point we highly recommend you spend a time walking along Regent Road there are some awesome cafes and restaurants as well as the famous Mojo market and of course we can't forget the proximity to the promenade probably our most favorite place in the whole of Cape Town the prom prom stretches for several kilometers along the coast and offers stunning views of the ocean and the city it's the perfect place to take a stroll a jog a cycle or a skate walking down down the prom you'll really get a feel for the vibe and energy of the city Sea point is good for digital Nomads active people basically it's got something for everyone cons of sea point and other places on the Atlantic Seaboard are firstly noise pollution you're right in the hustle and bustle especially if you stay on Main areas are very hilly and full of stairs making it a little harder to walk around and travel with big luggage moving further down the coast we get to Camps Bay and Bokoven a perfect place to stay for the ocean it can also be very bright and hot in the afternoons too so you will need to draw the blinds in your accommodation next is the traffic to get in and out of camps Bay can be a bit of a nightmare between the months of December and January and Ubers are also very difficult to come come by so be aware and then finally if you are near the main strip it can be a little busy and loud Bakoven is right next to camps Bay and is a premium neighborhood with some of our favorite beaches you won't find any hotels here it's mainly large villas up for rental on [Music] Airbnb now that the Atlantic Seaboard is done let's move our attention over to the V&A Waterfront and ForeShore areas the V&A is the Heart of the City for tourists it's a busling shopping and entertainment Hub with a wide range of luxury hotels and self-catering apartments it has stunning views of Table Mountain and is close to popular attractions like the two oceans aquarium and The Cape wheel it's an ideal base for those who want to be in the middle of the action and have access to dozens of great restaurants and the shopping mall do note the pricing for places to stay here in this area are definitely on the higher end some areas even have a bit of a Monaco yacht life going on things to do in the area include a Sunset Cruise Atlantic Outlook, eat at the restaurants take a boat trip from the harbor to Robben Island take a helicopter tour of the city and explore the markets and museums V&A is good for those that don't want to drive in the city Foodies and more mature Travelers the foreshore area is situated next to the V&A and on the border of the CBD you can find hotels such as the Westin, Southern sun and hotel sky in this area it is also really close to the international convention center and within walking distance to the V&A and the popular CBD streets of Bree and Loop which are filled with amazing restaurants and cafes do note the vibe here is definitely more busy so it will be busy during the week but it can feel like a bit of a dead zone on weekends the CBD is next on the list centrally located to basically all of the things to do that we've mentioned in this video and extremely walkable we love the array of cafes and restaurants in the area including the world renowned Fyn which is ranked in the world's top 50 restaurants the CBD is home to a number of budget Apartments like our loft apartment here at NeighborGoods many hostels and also luxury hotels like the Taj pepper Club Hotel in spa and the Hyatt Regency the most popular streets to stay near are Bree and Loop these streets are very vibey and filled with tons of different restaurants and cafes Long Street is very popular too and is known as the party and bar Street of the city now for the cons of staying in the CBD obviously it is very busy and as a result can be quite loud and overwhelming it's definitely not the place to be if you're looking for a more relaxed time although the CBD is and some of things to do the traffic and jay walking is pretty bad so it can take quite a while to get in and out of the center the amphitheater shaped City Bowl is next on the list we're currently at Signal Hill and we have beautiful views of the whole bowl behind us within it there are a number of really cool suburbs to stay in such as Gardens Tamboerskloof and Vredehoek the CBD is also technically part of the city Bowl but we've already covered that it is a popular choice for tourists with its central location and easy access to attractions like Table Mountain Signal Hill lion's head the vibey streets of Loop and Bree and the V&A Waterfront Gardens is probably the most popular suburb in the city bowl with the restaurant and Cafe filled Kloof Street being a focal point of the suburb accommodation options range from budget friendly guest houses some affordable hostels to home homey airbnbs if you're looking for a more chilled homey neighborhood Vibe with the options of great shops and cafes then Gardens is it Cons to staying in gardens and any of the surrounding neighborhoods is that they are surrounded by Devil's Peak table mountain lion's head and Signal Hill meaning during the morning and afternoons they can be a bit on the cooler side as they see less Sun the city bowl is also about a 15 to 20 minute drive from the nearest beaches if you love hiking though they are perfectly located we'll go over the other suburbs briefly Vredehoek and Oranjezicht are located here at the foot of Table Mountain they are quite Suburban with a lot of parks have great access to the mountain and stunning views over the entire city and table Bay Beyond do note this is one of the most windy areas to stay in Cape Town especially during the months of October to December you won't find as many hotels or hostels in these areas the main source of accommodation is going to be airbnbs Tamboerskloof is on the opposite side of the bowl, offering stunning views of Table Mountain you will have very easy access to hiking trails on lines head and Signal Hill as well as quick access to the city and the Kloof Street Cafe scene Below in terms of accommodation options here you will find a lot of charming Victorian and Edwardian style houses with beautiful high ceilings and spacious living areas Apartments town houses and some guest houses too next to Tamboerskloof is De Waterkant, known for its colorful houses and cobbled streets accommodation options here include guest houses boutique hotels and large condo buildings in terms of a location it is connected pretty well to Greenpoint and Sea Point and is 10 to 15 minutes from the beaches it is also bordering the vibey streets of Bree and Loop and within walking distance to the V&A Waterfront cons of staying in the De Waterkant though are that some of the Condo buildings especially those along Strand Street can suffer from quite a bit of noise and light pollution the city bowl is good for Cafe lovers and Foodies hikers budget Travelers and more artsy people moving out of the city to the southern suburbs or the leafy suburbs as locals call them due to the range of trees growing in the area Newlands Claremont Constantia and Bishop'sCourt are good for those looking for a quieter and more residential atmosphere Newlands is known for its beautiful gardens and parks and is close to the Kirstenbosch Botanical Gardens a stunning place to take a stroll or have a picnic if you stay in Newlands we can recommend you check out the Foresters Arms it's the oldest pub in South Africa overall the southern suburbs are a great location if you want to do some wine tasting at the Constanta wine estate the oldest estate in the country explore the botanical gardens or do some hikes up the back of Table Mountain we spent a couple weeks living in the southern suburbs and honestly it wasn't for us we found it a bit too far from the beaches we love to frequent it's also a lot cooler the days are shorter due to being in the shadow of the mountain and it rains a hell of a lot more it has a bit of a microclimate going on because it's on the windward side of the mountain in terms of accommodation options houses apartments and flats are very common so you'll find a lot of places at decent prices on Airbnb you won't find many hotels or hostels in these areas further out of the city there are a number of places to stay that we'll quickly mention Hout Bay has a variety of options is close to things to do such as the snorkeling with seals and the Chapman's Peak Drive Llandudno is a super lux suburb with million dollar Villas overlooking one of the most beautiful beaches in the country muizenberg is a laid-back Surfer town with a range of affordable accommodation options including Backpacker hostels and self-catering apartments the false Bay waters are also a lot warmer than those on the Atlantic Seaboard other towns further down like Kalk Bay and Simon town by Boulders Beach and the African penguins are very cute and worth staying in and offer for a very laid-back and quiet environment full of naturey things to do it's close to the Cape Point Nature Reserve with the waters down here being some of the most crystal blue we've ever seen Claire and I would love to retire here one day 25 minutes up the coast from the city is Blouberg a Beach town with amazing views of Table Mountain and lion's head Blouberg has some of the longest stretches of beach that is great for sunbathing kite surfing wind surfing and other water sports we have stayed in Blouberg before and again besides it being very windy at times we found it a bit too far from all the main attractions going on in the city and Atlantic Seaboard still it's a good option if you're looking for a cost-effective Beach holiday which still has decent access to the nearby Durbanvile Hills Wine State then lastly if being near the coast or city is not your Vibe at all we can recommend you looking to staying in some of the renowned wine valleys such as Stellenbosch or Franschoek you will be able to find various guest houses bed and breakfast and Heritage hotels with that iconic Cape Dutch architecture a lot of the wine Estates have accommodation options too there will also be a number of boutique hotels and self-catering cottages and Villas we highly recommend these two if you're looking for a more romantic Escape or a wine tasting Adventure in one of the most pictures landscapes in South Africa and that's it we've covered all the places we think you should consider when planning a trip to Cape Town again you can watch all of our other Cape Town videos for some amazing things to do and how to plan the perfect trip to the Mother City grab our resource pack available on our website which has links to multiple accommodations our favorite cafes and restaurants and so much more we hope you find this helpful and if you did we'd appreciate if you gave it a like it really helps more people discover the video we'll see you guys in the next one ciao

videos.transcript: [Applause] Welcome to Cape Town. Recently voted the number one city on Earth. Hanging with penguins on Boulders Beach. Getting to hang out with these tuxedo penguins on Boulder's Beach was by far our favorite experience we had in Cape Town. If you want these penguins all to yourself, then you need to get up bright and early to arrive at the entrance 30 minutes before it opens. We were in Cape Town during January, which is South Africa's summer. So, this beach opened at 7:30 a.m. So, we arrived at 7:00 a.m. sharp to be the first in line. A big tip here is to purchase your tickets online the night before, so you can go right in when you get here. It's around a 50-minute drive from Cape Town, so the best thing to do is hire a private driver. Renee can fit up to five people in his car, and if you want to book with him, you could scan this QR code on the screen right here. can bring clothes, food, drinks. If you love tequila, carry your tequila in the car. Go. Now, there are two entrances at Boulders Beach. Before visiting these boardwalks, you're going to want to head 2 minutes down the road to the other entrance, which is the actual Boulder Beach where you can hang with the penguins. If you want to go to this exact entrance, you can just type into Google Maps Middle Beach, and it will take you right to the parking lot. There will be a lot of people there every single morning, but most of them will be setting up on the beach for the rest of the day. What you want to do, though, is keep walking down the beach where you will see a tiny hole in the boulders. This right here is where you will crouch and crawl your way to the other side. But you are not done yet. You'll have to climb up and down rocks and walk through water until you arrive at Middle Beach. This is where you'll be greeted by the most hilarious penguins who will waddle right next to you and swim alongside you on the most stunning beach in the world. Please be respectful to these penguins. Don't touch them. Don't get too close to them. And please be chill so you don't scare them away. This is their home and you're a guest, so if you see anyone doing anything bad, please speak up and tell them to stop. On a real sad note though, these penguins are now critically endangered due to over fishing in South Africa. In the 1800s, there used to be 4.5 million. In the 1900s, that number dropped to 1.5 million. And today, that number is only 20 to 30,000 penguins left in the wild in the entire world. If the government doesn't come in and stop this over fishing, these penguins will be extinct by the year 2035. We're going to try and do our part by donating all the ad revenue from this video to Sangub, which is an agency trying to save these penguins in Cape Town and in South Africa. And if you want to donate yourself, you could scan the QR code on the screen right here. After hanging out at this beach for an hour or two, you can walk on the connected boardwalk for around 7 minutes to the main entrance and check out the entire colony. Even though you hung out with the penguins up close and personal on Middle Beach, you have to go see them from the boardwalks because there will be hundreds, if not thousands, waddling together and playing in the ocean, which is the cutest thing you will ever see. Now, let's move on to number two, the Cape of Good Hope. If you're on the tour with Renee, he will take you 30 minutes down the coast to the Cape of Good Hope. This is often mistaken as the most southern point in Africa, but that's actually 200 m down the coast in another spot. Nonetheless, this is a must do when you're in Cape Town because the views of the coastline are breathtaking, and you even get to see tons of different animals like these wild baboons that create a little bit of a traffic jam when we're there. There's also tons of seals, roaming ostriches, and if you're really lucky, you can even see zebras here. Fun fact, this is the exact spot in 1488 where Portuguese sailors discovered the sea route connecting Europe and Asia, which changed the world forever. We actually traveled all over Portugal for 3 months in our van. So, if you're interested in visiting, you should definitely click the link above to watch our entire Portugal series. The Cape of Good Hope is a UNESCO World Heritage site and has some of the most natural, untouched beauty I've seen in my entire life. An amazing thing to do up here is walk up 30 minutes to the lighthouse, which has 360° views of the coastline. And if you're really lucky, you can see whales from up here during whale season, which is between July and November. There is a restaurant up here right where you park called Two Oceans that we went to for lunch, but to be honest, they didn't have the best food, but the views you get from the balcony were just unbelievable. Maybe after hiking, you could stop here for coffee and a beer just to enjoy the views and then head somewhere else for lunch. To be honest, we only spent an hour and a half up here, but you can easily stay way longer because there are so many stunning beaches and lots of hiking. Now, up next, let's move on to number three, going on an African safari. I'm guessing if you came all the way to Africa, you want to live out your Lion King dreams of going on a safari. Luckily, there's a day trip from Cape Town where you can visit Aquila Game Reserve. Just a heads up, this is an all-day tour because it takes around 2 to 3 hours to drive there, but it's definitely worth it since you have the chance to see the big five: lions, leopards, rhinos, elephants, and buffalo. We didn't go on this exact safari because we hosted a group trip in South Africa with 12 strangers. It was so much fun. We did a road trip around the Garden Route and went to the Garden Route game lodge which is around 5 hours away from Cape Town. But I think no matter what it'll be a similar experience. If I'm going to be honest, you can't really go on a real safari near Cape Town. What I mean by that is it feels more like a very, very large natural zoo with fences that separate certain animals from each other. The only true safaris you can visit in South Africa are in the northeast corner of the country, right along the border of Mosmbique. Of course, the biggest and most iconic is Krueger National Park, but two others in that area that are just as amazing are Sabi Sands and Malamala. If you don't have time, I still think taking the day trip to see these amazing animals up close and personal is worth it. And if you want to go on the number one rated safari trip from Cape Town, you can scan this QR code on the screen right here. I think it's a great intro to these animals and a great motivation to go on more safaris all over Africa. What you learn on these safaris will stick with you for life and make you appreciate the world even more. Now, let's move on to number four. Snorkeling with seals. There's one thing in Cape Town that I think everyone should do, and that is snorkel with seals. Like Anna said before, we went on a garden route road trip. So, we ended up snorkeling in Platinburg Bay, which is around 6 hours away from Cape Town. But the experience will be pretty much the same here. I actually think the ocean safari could be a better experience because you spend four hours zooming around the coast, taking in the views, exploring hidden caves and shipwrecks, and of course swimming with the most playful animals in the world. And if you want to go on this adventure with the number one tour company in Hoot Bay, you could scan the QR code on the screen right here. Just a heads up though, the water around Cape Town is cold year round, but they will give you a thick wet suit, so you won't be too freezing. From this tour, you will leave from Hoot Bay, which is one of the most beautiful spots in all of Cape Town. Right off the coast from here is Dyker Island, which has thousands of wild Cape furs seals that will swim with you inside a kelp forest. I'm not kidding you, the way these animals swim up close to you and play around will go down as one of the most fun experiences I have done in my life. Now, let's move on to number five. Hiking Lion's Head or Clof Corner. Cape Town is known for its breathtaking sunsets, and by far the best way to take it in is by hiking to a viewpoint. The most well-known hike in all of Cape Town is Lion's Head. It's a threemile outand bath hike. It will take you around an hour to an hour and a half to get to the top with some sketchy parts, including some bouldering and ladders, so make sure to be careful and take your time. We didn't get around to doing Lion's Head ourselves, but our friends did and said it was beautiful, but also extremely packed. So, make sure to just keep that in mind. If you want an easier hike that still has amazing views, you can visit Clouff Corner. It's only a onem roundtrip hike and takes around 20 to 30 minutes to reach the top. The reason we love this hike is because you get amazing views of the coastline, sunset, and Lion's Head all at once. There'll still be a few people up here, but not nearly as crowded as the other one. And there's lots of spots to sit along the cliffside and take in the sunset. No matter which hike you choose, you have to do at least one of these when you're in Cape Town because the views are just breathtaking. Up next, we have number six, Clifton Beach. During our time in Cape Town, we visited a couple different beaches, including Camp's Bay. This is by far the most popular beach, but it was way too crowded. I will say if you're looking for lively areas with tons of restaurants, shopping, and bars, Camp's Bay is the way to go. But for a more quiet day, our favorite we went to was called Clifton Beach. It's actually four beaches that are separated by granite boulders. What I loved most about this beach is that it's sheltered from the wind. It has stunning blue water, white soft sand, and massive boulders scattered all around. Not to mention, Clifton is one of Cape Town's most luxurious suburbs, so you have many impressive properties on the cliffs above you and the massive Lion's Head Mountain as your backdrop. We were dropped off at Clinton's fourth beach and had an amazing time. It gets crowded here, too, but it didn't bother us as much because there's so much more room to find your spot as you walk down the beach. Also, parking can be really tough here, so it may be your best bet to get dropped off by an Uber. Another beach that's consistently voted number one in Cape Town is called Landudo, and it's only 20 minutes down the coast. Now, let's move on to number seven. Taking the cable car up to Table Mountain, you're going to see this iconic flattop mountain towering over Cape Town your entire trip. So, it's only right that you go to the top. Table Mountain is a UNESCO World Heritage site that is more than 260 million years old. And in 2011, it became one of the new seven wonders of nature alongside the Amazon rainforest. I highly recommend getting there early, right at 7:30 a.m., so you could be one of the first cable cars up when it opens at 8:00 a.m. This is another spot where you're going to want to purchase the tickets online the night before so you can go right up when you arrive. I'm going to be honest, the cable car ride up is worth it by itself because it zooms you up the mountain in 5 minutes and you have unbelievable views the entire time. On top of that, it spins around as you're making your way up, which is kind of trippy, too. When we went with our group trip during the middle of the day, we got very unlucky because the clouds were covering the entire top, so we didn't get any views at all. I'm going to overlay some footage of what it should look like. And wow, does it look insane. The only thing that made up for the clouds the top were these cute animals that lived up here called dassis. And it was our very first time ever seeing them. Believe it or not, they are the closest living relatives to elephants and manatees, which almost seems impossible to believe. Now, let's move on to number eight, Chapman's Peak. Voted one of the most beautiful roads in the world. Chapman's Peak is a 9 km or 5 1/2 mile road on the side of a cliff. The whole time you get unbelievable views of the Atlantic Ocean, stunning white sand beaches, and Hoot Bay. It was so cool seeing all the bikers whizzing down this narrow road. You can stop at many different viewpoints. If you're heading to Chapman's Peak, there is an amazing place to get food only 10 minutes away called Nord Hoke Farm Village, where they have so many different restaurants, markets, and of course, good vibes. Remember that driver, Renee, that took us all around Cape Town for the day? Well, he showed us this spot, and we were so happy he did because the food was delicious. Up next, we have number nine, shark cage diving. One of the most wild experiences you could do in Cape Town is go cage diving with sharks. This is one of the experiences we did with our group of 12 on our group trip. And I am going to be honest, people had mixed reviews about it. Just like the safari, to get to the sharks, you will have to drive 2 to 3 hours to a place called Hermanus. And if you want to go with the number one tour company that does this day trip out of Cape Town, you can scan the QR code on the screen right here. After some quick briefing, you will head to the boats and cruise around 20 minutes to the shark hot spot. You'll get your gear on and climb into this thin cage while the team members are baiting the sharks closer and closer to you. The reason some people didn't like it was because the cage was thin and it could feel a little claustrophobic and it was hard to stay underwater if you can't hold yourself down. I'm guessing that's why I ended up liking it so much because I could hold my breath underwater and hold myself down. So, I had a great chance to see so many sharks. I had no idea this was going to happen, but we also got to see these massive strings going after the bait and tons of seals swimming around. I feel like when I dreamed of going shark diving in South Africa, I always thought it was going to be with great white sharks. And sadly, it's super rare now to see them because two orcas went on a killing spree and killed so many great white sharks, which I thought was wild because I had no idea orcas could kill great whites so easily. Nonetheless, you will see all sorts of different sharks, and they're still quite big, so your heart will be pumping the entire time. Just a heads up for those of you that get boat sick, some people in our group threw up, so maybe try to take some medicine before coming out. Now, let's move on to number 10. Going to the wineries. Cape Town has been producing wine since 1652, which makes it the oldest wine producing region in the southern hemisphere. There are more than 500 wineries just in the Cape Town region alone. So, one of the best things to do is a winery hop for a day. Stellenbos and Franchook have the best wineries and they are only about an hour outside of Cape Town. I honestly think for most people the easiest and best choice here would be to take the tram and winery hop in Franchook. You could scan the QR code on the screen right here where a driver will bring you to the tram and provide you with the tickets. Just an FYI, this doesn't include the fees to visit each winery, but I think it's the most unique way to experience this region. There are five different tram routes to choose from that will take you to different wineries, and I've heard the very best routes are navy, red, and the orange lines. Sadly, the orange line is closed right now due to construction, but I bet it will open soon. So, just look for updates online. And for those of you watching that have been in this region, please leave your comment down below of what your favorite tram route was and also what your favorite wineries are in Cape Town. Like I said, even if you buy the tram ticket, you're still going to need to pay for the winery fees, which can vary from $3 all the way up to $14, which isn't too bad. Just please pace yourself because we've been on many wine tours around the world and have seen a lot of drunk people. May the odds ever be in your favor, my friends. [Applause]

videos.title: 15 Things we wish we knew before visiting. SOUTH AFRICA  🇿🇦
videos.description:
Planning a trip to South Africa? 🇿🇦 This is everything you need to know before you go.

After travelling through Cape Town, driving the Garden Route, and exploring beyond, we’ve put together 15 essential South Africa travel tips to help you avoid common mistakes and travel with confidence.

In this South Africa travel guide we cover:

• Unpredictable weather in South Africa
• Load shedding & power outages explained
• Using cards vs cash & tipping etiquette
• Why hiring a car in South Africa is a must
• Using Uber in Cape Town and major cities
• How much South Africa actually costs (food, accommodation & activities)
• The best types of accommodation (Airbnb vs hotels vs guesthouses)
• What to eat and drink in South Africa
• What to pack for a South Africa trip
• Can you drink tap water in South Africa?
• Is South Africa safe for tourists?
• Languages spoken in South Africa
• South African terminology you’ll hear
• Mosquitos, bugs & bites — malaria risk?
• South Africa plug sockets & adapters

Whether you’re planning a Cape Town itinerary, a Garden Route road trip, a South Africa safari, or visiting for the first time, this video will help you prepare properly.

South Africa is one of the most beautiful countries in the world — but there are a few things that might surprise you if you’re not prepared.

If you’re researching:
South Africa travel advice
South Africa travel tips 2026
Is South Africa safe to visit?
South Africa road trip guide
Cape Town travel guide
Garden Route travel tips
South Africa cost of travel

— this video will help.

📍 Locations featured: Cape Town, Garden Route & surrounding areas

Subscribe for honest travel guides, practical advice, and real costs.

videos.transcript: Here is a list of 15 things we wish we knew before traveling South Africa. We have just spent 6 weeks in this incredible country, but it wasn't all quite what we expected. So, let's get into it with number one, and that's the weather. The very unpredictable weather because microclimates are definitely a thing here. They get up to three times more rain on the eastern side of Table Mountain than the city side. and in Newlands on the backside of the mountain, it was kind of gray and a bit overcast almost every day. Whereas when we stayed in the city, there was nothing but clear blue skies. So, it is definitely a thing. And then when we were traveling along the Garden Route, we got some seriously rainy days along with some absolutely stunning sunny days. And the wind is definitely something to consider. So, we nearly got blown off of Table Mountain. we had a kayaking with penguin trip, which was actually canceled twice because of the winds. And unfortunately, we ran out of time, and it was really frustrating that we weren't able to rebook it. So, that leads me on to my first big tip. Schedule any outdoor activities as early on in your itinerary as possible. Most companies will allow you to reschedule if the weather is too bad on any particular day. And what you don't want to do is run out of time to do those absolutely fantastic activities. Things like the Robin Island ferry and the Table Mountain cable car are renowned for being canceled due to wind. Scheduling early will just give you the best chance of not missing out. Later, I'll also share some of the gear that we were really glad that we packed, and some of it that we'll definitely be adding next time that just would have made our outdoor activities that little bit more comfortable. Before our trip, we had heard a lot about load shedding. So, we picked accommodation with backup power or generators. But being here in early 2026, we actually didn't experience any. Our Airbnb hosts told us that it was far less common nowadays and if it's important to you, then backup power might just be something that you want to check with your accommodation. Cards are widely accepted here, but it's a good idea to carry a small amount of cash for tips and for parking attendants. So, parking attendants were a bit of a surprise to us at first, but you'll be able to spot them in their high-vis jackets and they will simply look after your car whilst you're gone, but expect a small tip, usually about 10 to 20 rand when you return. And then at petrol stations, I really loved that the attendants fill your tank for you and give your windows a little clean and again, a small tip is appreciated. In restaurants and bars, you'll see a couple of extra lines at the bottom of your bill. We were a bit confused what to do at first, but it's as simple as you just write in the amount that you want to tip and the amount of your total bill that you're going to pay. We usually went with 10 to 20% but given the overall cost of things and the chance to recognize helpful people, didn't mind at all. If you're comfortable driving, then we would definitely recommend hiring a car. It is by far the easiest way to see places outside of the city. Now, we're used to driving on the left in the UK, but there are a few other things that you should know. So, for example, it is illegal when you parallel park to park on the opposite side of the road and never park on a yellow line. And unfortunately, we can vouch for the fact that fines are strictly enforced. So, we actually went to see the penguins at Boulders Bay. There was an attendant that helped us park, but when we came back, there was a ticket flapping on the windscreen. It was at that point that we realized that we'd been directed to a yellow line. So, the moral of the story is don't always trust the parking attendants. But once you get used to these little differences, driving is really manageable and it's great that it gives you the opportunity to explore at your own pace and I'm saying that after being driven around by Ian for the last 6 weeks. >> We found Uber incredibly easy to use in Cape Town, the wine region, and some of the bigger towns along the Garden Route. It was cheap, reliable, and we felt comfortable using it. Just make sure that you download the app before you arrive at the airport. For us, it made that first journey from the airport into the city super easy. For us, coming from the UK, South Africa felt incredibly good value for money. With the exception of one thing, which I'll come to in a moment. >> Everyday things like the Ubers and good coffees were much cheaper than we're used to, and even a good meal out in a restaurant with a couple of drinks felt really good value for money. And bigger experiences like the Robin Island tour also felt good value for money for what you were getting. So, the one exception was the entry fees for some of the bigger national parks. Cape Point, for example, was 515 rand, the equivalent of 25 pounds, which initially felt a bit steep. However, after arriving and realizing that you could spend the whole day there exploring, the hiking, taking a picnic, some even had beaches. At the end of the day, it did feel like it was better value for money than we had originally thought.
